stem: Let \( f(x,y) = [[A]]x^{2} + [[B]]xy + [[C]]y^{2} + [[D]]x + [[E]]y \). What is the value of the Laplacian \( f_{xx} + f_{yy} \) at \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \)? (\( f \) is harmonic there exactly when this value is 0.)
solution: Here \( f_{xx}=2[[A]] \) and \( f_{yy}=2[[C]] \) (both constant), so \( f_{xx}+f_{yy} = 2[[A]]+2[[C]] = [[answer]] \) at every point, including \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the factor of 2 from differentiating the squared terms.
This subtracts instead of adds the two second partials.
This mistakenly includes the cross-term coefficient \( [[B]] \), which does not appear in \( f_{xx} \) or \( f_{yy}\).
This reports the cross-term coefficient itself, not the Laplacian.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x,y) = [[A]]x^{4} + [[B]]y^{2} \). What is the value of the Laplacian \( f_{xx} + f_{yy} \) at \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \)? (\( f \) is harmonic there exactly when this value is 0.)
distractor_rationales: This uses the first derivatives \( f_x, f_y \) instead of the second derivatives.
This subtracts instead of adds the two second partials.
This forgets to square \( x_0 \) in the \( f_{xx} \) term.
This drops the \( f_{yy} \) contribution entirely.
This is off by four from the correct value.
This is off by four from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x,y) = [[A]]e^{x}\cos(y) \). What is the value of the Laplacian \( f_{xx} + f_{yy} \) at \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \)? (\( f \) is harmonic there exactly when this value is 0.)
solution: Here \( f_{xx} = [[A]]e^{x}\cos(y) \) and \( f_{yy} = -[[A]]e^{x}\cos(y) \), so \( f_{xx}+f_{yy}=0 \) at every point, including \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \): \( f \) is harmonic everywhere. The value is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This mistakenly uses \( f_{yy}=+[[A]]e^{x}\cos(y) \) instead of the correct \( -[[A]]e^{x}\cos(y)\), so the terms double instead of cancelling.
This reports only \( f_{xx}\), forgetting to add \( f_{yy}\).
This differentiates \( \cos(y) \) only once instead of twice, giving a \( \sin \) term.
This differentiates \( \cos(y) \) only once (with the wrong sign) instead of twice.
This assumes the Laplacian is a nonzero constant instead of correctly cancelling to zero.
This assumes the Laplacian is a nonzero constant instead of correctly cancelling to zero.

stem: Let \( f(x,y) = [[A]]x^{3} - 3[[A]]xy^{2} \). What is the value of the Laplacian \( f_{xx} + f_{yy} \) at \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \)? (\( f \) is harmonic there exactly when this value is 0.)
solution: Here \( f_{xx} = 6[[A]]x \) and \( f_{yy} = -6[[A]]x \), so \( f_{xx}+f_{yy}=0 \) at every point, including \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \): \( f \) (the real part of \( [[A]](x+iy)^{3} \)) is harmonic everywhere. The value is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This mistakenly uses \( f_{yy}=+6[[A]]x \) instead of the correct \( -6[[A]]x\), so the terms double instead of cancelling.
This reports only \( f_{xx}\), forgetting to add \( f_{yy}\).
This reports only \( -f_{yy}\)'s coefficient pattern applied to \( y_0 \) rather than the correct expression in \( x_0 \).
This mixes \( x_0 \) and \( y_0 \) incorrectly instead of using \( f_{xx}, f_{yy}\) both in terms of \( x_0 \) alone.
This assumes the Laplacian is a nonzero constant instead of correctly cancelling to zero.
This assumes the Laplacian is a nonzero constant instead of correctly cancelling to zero.
